coredata.dc:title: Rapid microwave synthesis of N-doped carbon nanodots with high fluorescence brightness for cell imaging and sensitive detection of iron (III)
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Xianfeng
Lu, Jingbo
Zhou, Xiaoli
Guo, Chunyan
Wang, Chuanhu
coredata.dc:description: We rapidly prepared N-doped photoluminescent carbon nanodots (CNDs) with the one-step microwave irradiation method using diammonium hydrogen citrate as the carbon source. The as-prepared CNDs possessed quasispherical morphology and a high quantum yield of about 26.8%, which was higher than the CNDs obtained by most other microwave-assisted methods. Moreover, the luminescent CNDs could be efficiently uptaken by BGC-823 cells and CT26.WT cells, and exhibited low cytotoxicity and favorable biocompatibility, making them suitable candidates for cell imaging. In addition, the CNDs could be utilized for Fe3+ ions sensitive detection with a detection limit of 180 nM.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon nanodots
Photoluminescence
Cell imaging
Fe3+ ions detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Effects of pH and metal ions on the photoluminescence of CNDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration for microwave irradiation synthesis of N-doped CNDs.
(a) TEM image and (b) diameter distribution histogram of the synthesized CNDs. The black line is the Gaussian fitting curve. Inset is HRTEM image (scale bar is 2 nm) of a single CNDs particle.
FTIR spectra of PEG-passivated CNDs (a), passivation-free CNDs (b) and diammonium hydrogen citrate (c).
(a) XPS, (b) C1s, (c) N1s, and (d) O1s spectra of the obtained CNDs.

(a) UV–vis absorption, excitation (emission at 432 nm), and emission (excited at 370 nm) spectra of the synthesized CNDs (inset: photographs of CNDs solution taken under visible light and 365 nm UV light). (b) Fluorescence spectra of the synthesized CNDs at different excitation wavelengths from 330 nm to 390 nm.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of CNDs upon the addition of NaCl solution of different concentrations. (b) Fluorescence spectra of CNDs upon the addition of phosphate buffers with different pH values. (c) Dependence of fluorescence intensity on 365 nm UV light excitation time for CNDs in deionized water.

(a) Fluorescence normalization intensities of CNDs upon the addition of different metal ions (10 mM). (b) Fluorescence spectra of CNDs upon the addition of Fe3+ ions of different concentrations. (c) The dependence of F/F
0 on the Fe3+ concentrations within the range of 0–1.5 mM. (d) Plot of F/F
0 as a function of Fe3+ concentrations.

Confocal fluorescence images of (a–c) BGC-823 cells and (d–f) CT26.WT cells incubated with CNDs (1 mg mL−1) for 24 h. Images obtained under (a, d) bright field and (b, e) excitation wavelength of 405 nm, and (c, f) the merged images of above.
Quantum yield of the CNDs samples.

head.ce:title: Rapid microwave synthesis of N-doped carbon nanodots with high fluorescence brightness for cell imaging and sensitive detection of iron (III)

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Material and Chemical Engineering, Bengbu University, Bengbu, 233030, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Material and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Anhui Provincial Engineering Research Center for Polysaccharide Drugs, Department of Pharmacy, Wannan Medical College, Wuhu, 241000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Anhui Provincial Engineering Research Center for Polysaccharide Drugs

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We rapidly prepared N-doped photoluminescent carbon nanodots (CNDs) with the one-step microwave irradiation method using diammonium hydrogen citrate as the carbon source. The as-prepared CNDs possessed quasispherical morphology and a high quantum yield of about 26.8%, which was higher than the CNDs obtained by most other microwave-assisted methods. Moreover, the luminescent CNDs could be efficiently uptaken by BGC-823 cells and CT26.WT cells, and exhibited low cytotoxicity and favorable biocompatibility, making them suitable candidates for cell imaging. In addition, the CNDs could be utilized for Fe3+ ions sensitive detection with a detection limit of 180 nM.

Facile microwave synthesis of N-doped carbon nanodots with high fluorescence brightness for cellular imaging.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent probes with prominent optical properties could be used for future cell biology and biomedical applications, such as biodetection, bioimaging, and disease diagnosis [1,2]. Fluorescent carbon nanodots (CNDs) are members of the family of carbon nanomaterials, and have a lateral size of less than 10 nm. CNDs have attracted tremendous attention in the fields of biolabeling, biomedicine, sensors, photocatalysis, optoelectronic devices, and so on [3–6]. Compared to commonly used probes, such as traditional organic dyes and semiconductor quantum dots (QDs), CNDs are promising candidates, because of their aqueous solubility, high photostability, robust chemical inertness, easy functionalization, excellent photobleaching resistance, low toxicity, and good biocompatibility [7–10]. There are various methods that can be used to prepare CNDs, such as the hydrothermal method [11], laser ablation [12], electrochemistry [13], ultrasonic vibration [14], microwave radiation [15], and so on. However, current some methods for preparing CNDs are complex, or quantum yields of prepared CNDs are generally low, which greatly limit its application in the fields of cell imaging and biodetection as a fluorescent probe.

At present, microwave radiation has been regarded as the most effective, inexpensive, and rapid method to synthesize fluorescent CNDs, because it is easy to perform, does not require much equipment or many instruments, and saves time [10]. The CNDs passivated by organic solvent can effectively improve CNDs' fluorescence quantum yield, the possible mechanism of which is that the passivation agent may fill the surface defects of CNDs, or the present of surface energy traps may lead to visible luminescence of CNDs [12]. Doping is also an effective method for improving quantum yield of CNDs, because of doping creating n-type and p-type carriers, which change the electronic structures of CNDs [8]. The synthesis and passivation of CNDs could be completed in one step during microwave irradiation, making it an economical and environmentally friendly method for the large-scale production of highly fluorescent CNDs.

Herein, we rapidly prepared the N-doped fluorescent CNDs with the one-step microwave irradiation method, as shown in Fig. 1 , using diammonium hydrogen citrate as the carbon source containing nitrogen and poly(ethylene glycol) (PEG-400) as the passivation reagent, without adding any other acid or alkaline solvent. The as-prepared CNDs had quasispherical morphology with a high quantum yield of about 26.8%, which was higher than the CNDs obtained by most other microwave-assisted methods [15–18]. In addition, the N-doped CNDs could be utilized for Fe3+ ions sensitive detection, with a detection limit of 180 nM, and for cell imaging as label-free fluorescent nanoprobes. Additionally, the one-step synthesis process of CNDs is facile, efficient, and cost-effective, and could potentially allow for large-scale production.

Diammonium hydrogen citrate, disodium hydrogen citrate, PEG-400, and dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). Citric acid, ammonium oxalate, and ammonium acetate were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. (China). 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) was obtained from Sigma-Aldrich. The Roswell Park Memorial Institute (RPMI)-1640 medium was supplied by Gibco Life Technologies (Shanghai, China). The semipermeable membranes were obtained from Shanghai Yuanye Biological Technology Co., Ltd. The BGC-823 and CT26.WT cells were purchased from the Institute of Biochemistry and Cell Biology (Shanghai, China). Other chemicals were of analytical grade and used as received. Deionized water with a resistivity of 18.1 MΩ cm was used throughout the experiments.

In a typical microwave-assisted synthesis procedure, 0.4 g of diammonium hydrogen citrate and 10 mL of PEG-400 were added to 10 mL of deionized water to form a transparent solution, and then heated in a domestic microwave oven (750 W, Galanz, China) for 6 min. The color of the solution gradually changed from transparent and colorless to wine red, and then to dark brown. When the reaction mixture was cooled down to room temperature, the CNDs solution was dialyzed with the semipermeable membrane (MWCO = 500) for 48 h. The solution was freeze-dried after dialysis, weighed, dissolved in deionized water, and used for cytotoxicity and cell imaging measurements.

The size and morphology of N-doped CNDs were observed using high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) on a JEM-2100 (JEOL, Japan). For TEM imaging, 10 μL of CNDs suspension was deposited on a carbon-coated copper grid and dried under room temperature. Fourier transformed infrared (FTIR) spectra were acquired over the range of 400–4000 cm−1 on a Nicolet 380 FTIR spectrometer (Thermo, USA). For IR analysis, a sample was dispersed in KBr and pressed into a sheet with appropriate pressure. A total of 32 scans were accumulated with a resolution of 4 cm−1 for each spectrum. X-Ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were obtained using a Thermo Scientific ESCALAB 250Xi with Multitechnique Surface Analysis. The photoluminescence spectra were collected on a Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer (Varian, USA) equipped with a Xe lamp as the light source. UV–vis absorption spectrum of CNDs was acquired on a U-3900 spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan), using a 1 cm path length cuvette.

The quantum yield(φ) of the N-doped CNDs was estimated by comparing the integrated luminescence intensities (excited at 360 nm) and the absorbance values (at 360 nm) of the CNDs with that of the quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4(φ = 0.54). Specifically, the absorbance values of CNDs and quinine sulfate were measured at 360 nm. Fluorescence spectra of CNDs and quinine sulfate were also recorded at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm to obtain the integrated luminescence intensity, which is the area under the photoluminescence emission curve in the wavelength range from 380 to 700 nm. The quantum yield of the CNDs was calculated using the following equation:
φ x
=
φ
s t
×
I x
I
s t
×
A
s t
A x
×
η x 2
η
s t
2
,
in which the subscripts st and x denoted quinine sulfate standard and CNDs, respectively, φ and I were the fluorescence quantum yield and integrated emission intensity, and A and η were the optical density and refractive index (both are 1.33). To minimize the reabsorption effects, we kept the absorbance at 360 nm in a 1 cm cuvette below 0.05.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of pH and metal ions on the photoluminescence of CNDs
ce:section.ce:para: Here, 40 μL of synthesized CNDs (10 mg mL−1) was added in 3200 μL of phosphate buffers with different pH values. The corresponding photoluminescence spectra of CNDs at different pH were then acquired upon excitation at 370 nm. Various metal ions, including Na+, K+, NH4 +, Ag+, Pb2+, Cu2+, Ba2+, Fe2+, Ca2+, Ni2+, Zn2+, Al3+, Y3+, Eu3+, and Fe3+ in aqueous solutions (10 mM) were first prepared. Then, 40 μL of synthesized CNDs (10 mg mL−1) was diluted with 3200 μL of each metal ion solution. The mixed solutions were incubated for 5 min. Then, the fluorescence spectra of CNDs in the absence and presence of various metal ions were recorded upon a 370 nm excitation.

BGC-823 and CT26.WT cells were cultured in a RPMI-1640 medium supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum, 1% penicillin, and 1% amphotericin at 37 °C in a 5% CO2 saturated humidity incubator. The BGC-823 and CT26.WT cells were seeded in 96-well plates at a density of 1 × 104 per well. The cells were cultured in a CO2 incubator for 24 h, and then, 100 μL of CNDs solution were added into the wells with different concentration ranges from 0 to 1 mg mL−1 and incubated for 24 h. Subsequently, 10 μL of MTT solution was added and incubated with the cells for an additional 4 h. Then, the medium was replaced with 200 μL/well of DMSO, and the culture plate was shaken for 15 min to completely dissolve the crystals. Finally, the optical density (OD) of each well was immediately measured at 490 nm using a model 550 microplate reader (Bio-Rad, USA). The experiment was repeated at least 3 times. The cell survival rates in the presence of CNDs of various concentrations were calculated according to the following formula:
Cell
viability% =
OD
treated
/
OD
control
× 100 % .

The BGC-823 and CT26.WT cells were inoculated in the culture dish, and incubated with the RPMI-1640 medium for 12 h at 37 °C in 5% CO2. 1 mL of CNDs (1 mg mL−1) were added into a cells solution and incubated for 24 h. Then the medium was removed and the cells were washed with phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) three times, and observed under Leica SP8 (Leica, Germany) laser scanning confocal fluorescence microscopy.

Microwave irradiation technology is a promising alternative because of its in-situ, transient heating properties and large-scale synthesis of homogeneous CNDs. Microwave irradiation time played an important role in the preparation of fluorescent CNDs. The results showed that the fluorescence emission of CNDs increased in intensity, as the microwave irradiation time increased, and reached a maximum when the carbon source was heated for 6 min in the microwave oven. This was followed by a decrease upon a further increase of irradiation time, concomitant with a gradual red shift of the fluorescence emission peak from 431 nm to 438 nm (Fig. S1). This was because a longer microwave irradiation time could lead to a higher graphitization degree of CNDs and the formation of large-sized CNDs that had poor photoluminescence [12,15,19] (Fig. S2). The carbon source solution remains transparent during the first 2 min of microwave irradiation, which is almost consumed by the evaporation of water. When microwave irradiation is longer than 2 min, the diammonium hydrogen citrate molecules are aromatized by intramolecular dehydration with a “polymerization” process, and the reaction solution turn to wine red. Next the intermediates are carbonized to form carbon nuclei of CNDs [20], and the solution become dark brown 3 min later (Fig. S3). The carbon nuclei subsequently form nanocrystalline CNDs passivated by PEG-400. To verify the critical role of the passivation agent PEG-400 in the preparation of fluorescent CNDs, we conducted the control experiments of PEG-400 alone and in the absence of PEG-400, which resulted in the formation of light yellow solution and wine red solution with no or weak photoluminescence, respectively (Figs. S4 and S5). However, the CNDs, which we prepared by adding PEG-400 under the same conditions, had strong luminescence intensity and the solution color of the CNDs was dark brown (Figs. S4 and S5). Moreover, the infrared spectra of PEG-400 before and after microwave irradiation were investigated. There is no obvious change in infrared characteristic absorption peak position (Fig. S6), suggesting the PEG-400 would not be decomposed or be carbonized under microwave reaction condition. This indicated that the observed bright fluorescence emission could be due to the surface-passivated CNDs rather than PEG-400 itself. As another key factor, the carbon source concentration could also influence the photoluminescence properties of the prepared CNDs. The luminescence intensity of CNDs was almost the same when the carbon source concentration ranged from 15 to 25 mg mL−1, but decreased when the carbon source concentration was further increased (Fig. S7). Thus, 20 mg mL−1 of carbon source and microwave irradiation for 6 min was chosen as the optimal condition for the synthesis of CNDs.

The synthesized CNDs appeared as quasispherical nanoparticles with good monodispersity in the TEM image (Fig. 2 a), and the Gaussian fitting curve revealed that the average size of CNDs was approximately 2.8 ± 0.7 nm, by calculating them from one hundred nanoparticles (Fig. 2b). The HRTEM image of CNDs revealed the distinct lattice structure with lattice spacing about 0.21 nm in Fig. 2a, indicating their highly crystalline. To further explore the structure and functional groups on the surface of CNDs, we collected the FTIR spectrum of CNDs. A broad infrared absorption band at 3480 and 3153 cm−1 was due to the O−H and N−H stretching vibrations of the surface hydroxyl and amine groups (Fig. 3 ) [21]. We assigned the peaks at 1719 cm−1 to CO stretching vibrations. The characteristic bending vibration of the amide II N−H bond was at 1568 cm−1 [11]. The bands at 1400 and 1219 cm−1 were related to the bending vibration of the C−O bond and the stretching vibration of the C−O−C bond, respectively. CNDs with amounts of hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on their surface possessed excellent water solubility, which could be used in cell imaging and drug delivery. The peak at 1603 cm−1 disappear in PEG-passivated CNDs when compared to passivation-free CNDs indicates chemical interaction between hydroxyl groups of PEG and carboxyl groups on the surface of CNDs (Fig. 3). The FTIR spectrum of diammonium hydrogen citrate is also shown in Fig. 3. The vibration position of the two different chemical environment carboxyl groups at 1692 and 1603 cm−1 in diammonium hydrogen citrate were changed into the CO stretching vibration under microwave irradiation conditions. The XPS spectra measurement was also performed to confirm the composition of CNDs. Fig. 4 a revealed three peaks at 284.93 eV, 400.38 eV, and 531.43 eV, which are attributed to C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively [22,23], and the ratio of C, N and O in the CNDs to be 68.65%, 13.81% and 17.55%, respectively. The C1s spectra (Fig. 4b) exhibited four peaks at 284.5 eV, 285.9 eV, 288.1 eV, and 288.8 eV, which are attributed to carbon in the form of C–C (sp3), C–N (sp3), C=O (sp2), and O–C=O (sp2), respectively [22,23]. The N1s peaks at 400.3 eV and 402.3 eV shown in Fig. 4c indicate that nitrogen exists mostly in the form of (C)–N (sp3) and N–H (sp3), respectively [24]. This verifies that the as-prepared CNDs are N-doped CNDs. The O1s spectra (Fig. 4d) showed two peaks at 531.3 eV and 532.3 eV, respectively [24], which are associated with oxygen in the states of C=O and C–OH/C–O–C. The results are consistent with the corresponding FTIR analysis.

We also investigated the photoluminescence response of N-doped CNDs at different ionic strengths, which were controlled by various concentrations of NaCl, the pH balances of solutions, and UV light exposure times. The Fluorescence spectra of the CNDs remained almost unchanged as the NaCl concentrations increased from 0 to 1.0 M (Fig. 6 a), which was significant, because CNDs could keep strong photoluminescence under physical salt concentrations in practical applications. The pH-dependent photoluminescence behavior was also investigated. Interestingly, the synthesized CNDs possessed a pH-sensitive fluorescence property. As illustrated in Fig. 6b, the luminescence intensities decreased in a solution of higher or lower pH, but remained at larger values in a solution of neutral conditions and weak alkalinity (7 ≤ pH ≤ 11). This pH-dependent fluorescence arose from the protonation and deprotonation of carboxyl or amino groups on the surface of the CNDs, which caused electrostatic doping/charging of the CNDs and shifted the Fermi level [26,27]. However, we found that slight photobleaching occurred after 100 min of continuous 365 nm UV light excitation (Fig. 6c). This result might have occurred because of the chemical structure of CNDs with graphite lattices [11,15,28]. The above experimental results showed that the synthesized CNDs' excellent resistance to salt and optical stability made them suitable for long-term detection of fluorescent probes in cells and in vivo.

High fluorescent CNDs could serve as a sensing candidate, because the oxygen-containing groups on their surfaces could coordinate with transition metal ions. We investigated the fluorescence quenching effects of various metal ions on CNDs. We added different metal ions (Na+, K+, NH4 +, Ag+, Pb2+, Cu2+, Ba2+, Fe2+, Ca2+, Ni2+, Zn2+, Al3+, Y3+, Eu3+, and Fe3+), each at a concentration of 10 mM, into a 10 mg mL−1 CNDs dispersion. Fig. 7 a shows the fluorescence spectra of the different metal ions and blank solution after the synthesized CNDs were added for 5 min under normalization. It was obvious that the Fe3+ ion had the greatest effect on fluorescence quenching among all of the tested metal ions. The mechanism of Fe3+ ion quenching could mainly be ascribed to the electron or energy transfer process occurring among the excited CNDs [29]. We measured the fluorescence spectra of the aqueous solution of CNDs, upon the addition of Fe3+ of different concentrations. We observed that the fluorescence intensity at 432 nm decreased when the Fe3+ concentration increased, ranging from 0 μM to 1.5 mM (Fig. 7b). The dependence of F/F
0 on the Fe3+ concentrations is further shown in Fig. 7c, where F
0 and F represent the fluorescence intensities of CNDs at 432 nm in the absence and presence of Fe3+ ions, respectively. There was a linear relationship between the F/F
0 and Fe3+ ions concentrations in the range of 0–100 μM (F/F
0 = 0.970–0.006c[Fe3+]; R
2 = 0.9932) (Fig. 7d), and the detection limit was estimated to be 180 nM (S/N = 3). For the Pb2+, Cu2+, Fe2+, and Al3+ ions, the slight fluorescence quenching could be attributed to the nonspecific interactions between the carboxylic groups and the metal ions.

Prior to evaluating the potential of CNDs in cell imaging, we investigated the cytotoxicity of the synthesized CNDs using the MTT assay, specifically, by incubating BGC-823 cells and CT26.WT cells in the CNDs solution with the concentration range of 0.1–1.0 mg mL−1 for 24 h. The experimental results showed that cell viability of >93% (BGC-823) and >90% (CT26.WT) were achieved at a CNDs concentration of <0.8 mg mL−1. The viability of cells still remained at >91% for BGC-823 cells and at >82% for CT26.WT cells in the presence of CNDs at a concentration level of 1.0 mg mL−1 (Fig. S9). The CNDs are promising candidates for living cell labeling and imaging, because of their unique photoluminescence properties, low cytotoxicity, and favorable biocompatibility. The BGC-823/CT26.WT cell imaging results, following incubation with CNDs (1 mg mL−1) for 24 h, are shown in Fig. 8 . Bright blue emissions were clearly recorded on the fluorescent microscope with laser excitation at 405 nm, while no visible fluorescence emission was detected in the control experiments (Fig. S10). This observation suggested that the CNDs were uptaken by BGC-823 cells and CT26.WT cells. Notably, we did not observe any morphological damage in the cells, which further confirmed the low cytotoxicity of the CNDs. This thereby demonstrated that the synthesized CNDs with highly fluorescence brightness were promising candidates as optical nanoprobes for cell imaging applications.

In summary, we rapidly synthesized N-doped highly fluorescent carbon nanodots with quasispherical morphology within 6 min via microwave irradiation, using diammonium hydrogen citrate as the carbon source. The synthesized carbon nanodots possessed excellent resistance to salt, optical stability, and excellent water solubility with light yellow color in the daylight and a bright blue color under 365 nm UV light. MTT assay and confocal fluorescence imaging measurements showed that the N-doped carbon nanodots exhibited low cytotoxicity toward BGC-823 cells and CT26.WT cells, suggesting that they were suitable for long-term detection and imaging as fluorescent probes in cells and in vivo. In addition, the synthesized carbon nanodots could be used for Fe3+ ions sensitive detection with concentrations in the range of 0–100 μM.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21503004), Natural Science Foundation of Anhui Province (No. 1508085QB32), the Excellent Young Talents Foundation for Colleges and Universities of Anhui Province (No. gxyqZD2016357), Natural Science Foundation of Anhui Province's Higher Education of China (No. KJ2015A205 and No. 113052015KJ10), and the Key Discipline Construction Project of Applied Chemistry of Bengbu University.

ce:section.ce:para: The following is the supplementary data related to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.optmat.2016.11.026.
